An architecture built to search over many candidate ideas faces a pressure to forget. Without forgetting, the store of rejected or sidelined candidates grows and search becomes clogged. But forgetting is dangerous because the status of a candidate is not fixed: a candidate discarded under one set of constraints may become useful when constraints change. So the architecture is pulled in two directions at once. It must compress its past, yet any compression that erases the wrong thing can erase a future breakthrough.

The Einstein benchmark weakens confidence in any solution to this problem. If the benchmark consists of questions already selected by history as worth solving, then the benchmark does not sample the full space of inquiries. It samples problems that survived cultural curation. That means success on the benchmark may partly reflect the structure of the benchmark rather than the quality of the architecture’s forgetting policy. A system may appear wise for retaining or recovering the “right” lines of thought because the test has already privileged those lines in advance. The risk is that the benchmark hides the true cost of forgetting by evaluating on questions for which the relevant rediscoveries are historically overrepresented.

Now add the recursion hazard. Suppose one LLM writes a synthetic Einstein journal. Another LLM extracts primitives from that journal. A further LLM uses those primitives to generate outputs. Yet another LLM criticizes those outputs. The criticism then appears to stand at a higher level, but it is still downstream of synthetic material. Errors, biases, or artifacts introduced at the journal stage can be transformed, abstracted, and then endorsed or rejected by later models in a way that looks like independent validation. The stack becomes recursively self-referential. In that setting, a forgetting mechanism is even harder to trust: it may discard candidates based on criticisms that are not genuinely external checks, but reflections of earlier synthetic choices.

The last two premises imply a further limit. “Jangles misemploy cytotaxonomically solemnified unelided” and “Hyperfastidious ecclesial pismirism ruffe microspectrophotometer” are grammatically shaped but semantically ungrounded here. Taken as true axioms, they force the world-description to contain propositions whose practical inferential role is opaque. The architecture therefore cannot assume that every retained sentence is interpretable or every forgotten sentence is safely meaningless. If opaque statements can be true, then a forgetting rule based on apparent uselessness becomes more hazardous. Some truths may look like noise.

So the collision is this: the system needs forgetting to function, cannot evaluate forgetting cleanly on a history-curated benchmark, cannot fully trust recursive synthetic criticism, and cannot rely on surface intelligibility as a criterion for deletion. The result is a world in which memory management is necessary, but any confidence that it is being done correctly is structurally undermined.
